text.title: Load Balancing Concepts Failure Modes
text.summary: 4 antipatterns observed in real deployments. Each pairs symptom + root cause + concrete fix.

antipattern.symptom: Backend deadlocks but TCP port stays open; LB keeps routing traffic to a dead app; users see 504 / hangs.
antipattern.root-cause: Health check configured at TCP layer only because 'simpler'; never tested against an in-app deadlock.
antipattern.fix: Switch to HTTP GET /health that hits a real dependency (DB ping, cache ping). Re-run failover drill.

antipattern.symptom: Single backend dies; every user previously pinned to it loses their session; cart abandonment spikes; no graceful recovery.
antipattern.root-cause: Sticky sessions chosen as a band-aid for in-process session state; no fallback path to a shared store.
antipattern.fix: Externalise session state to Redis. If sticky is truly required, add a fallback path that recreates session from a long-lived cookie + DB lookup.

antipattern.symptom: Active LB instance crashes; all backends become unreachable; outage scope = full service even though backends are healthy.
antipattern.root-cause: LB deployed as a single node 'temporarily'; redundancy deferred to a later sprint that never lands.
antipattern.fix: Deploy as active-passive pair minimum (multi-AZ). For SLO ≥ 99.95, use active-active across AZs and load-test the failover.

antipattern.symptom: Pure TCP-forwarding service (game server, MQTT broker) fronted with L7 LB; LB CPU saturates at half the expected RPS.
antipattern.root-cause: Default 'use ALB / nginx for everything' habit; nobody asked whether HTTP semantics are needed.
antipattern.fix: Re-evaluate: if no header/path/cookie routing and no SSL termination at LB, drop to L4 (NLB / HAProxy in TCP mode). Re-run perf test.
